items.title: Why Mattering Matters | Rebecca Newberger Goldstein
items.author: Closer To Truth
items.content:
<article>*What is the most distinctively human drive — and what happens when we get it wrong?* Philosopher and novelist Rebecca Newberger Goldstein explores the “mattering instinct”: the human need to feel that our self-importance is objectively warranted. Drawing on physics, biology, Spinoza, and decades of philosophical inquiry, she explains how this longing shapes our lives, divides us, and points toward what a well-lived life may require.

0:00 Why Mattering Matters
1:57 What It Means to Matter
6:39 Matter, Entropy, and Life
12:48 Becoming Human
14:49 Eudaimonia vs. Happiness
17:21 The Four Mattering Strategies
22:38 Transcendent Mattering
25:27 Competitive Mattering
27:36 Getting Mattering Right and Wrong
33:57 Achievement and Atrocity

*Rebecca Newberger Goldstein* is a MacArthur Fellow and National Humanities Medal recipient whose ten acclaimed books span philosophy and fiction, including her new work _The Mattering Instinct: How Our Deepest Longing Drives Us and Divides Us._

items.title: Can Any Religion Claim Truth? | William Grassie
items.author: Closer To Truth
items.content:
<article>*With so many conflicting religions, can any one claim to be true?* Scholar of comparative religion William Grassie explores how diverse belief systems address the same fundamental human questions while arriving at radically different answers—and whether these differences reflect cultural "languages" expressing shared concerns, or challenge any religion's claim to exclusive truth.

0:00 The Problem of Conflicting Religions
0:30 A Universalist Approach
1:09 Religion as Cultural Language
2:45 Same Questions, Different Answers
3:23 Diversity Within Religions
4:22 Religions and Ultimate Reality
7:08 The Problem with Exclusivism

William John Grassie is an American interdisciplinary scholar, social activist, and organizational consultant. He received his doctorate in comparative religion from Temple University and his BA in political science from Middlebury College. A leader in the interdisciplinary and international dialogue between science and religion, Grassie is at work on a book-length study of the evolutionary phase in which human cultural processes begin to dominate the biological development of the planet.

items.title: The AI Containment Problem | Roman Yampolskiy
items.author: Closer To Truth
items.content:
<article>*Can a superintelligent AI ever truly be contained?*

AI safety researcher Roman Yampolskiy argues that sufficiently advanced systems are not just dangerous but inherently uncontainable. In this conversation, he breaks down why virtual boxes fail, how AI agents develop self-preservation drives through Darwinian selection — without being programmed to — and what it means that we're already moving from tools to autonomous agents at scale.

If containment is impossible, what follows?

00:00 The Triad of Risks: Unexplainable, Unpredictable, Uncontrollable
00:44 Defining Control: Direct vs. Delegated
01:24 The Containment Problem: Why Virtual Boxes Fail
02:30 Darwinian Drives in AI: Self-Preservation and Resource Acquisition
03:40 From Tools to Agents: The Rise of Self-Motivating AI
05:25 The Global Impact: A Million Uncontrolled Agents
06:26 Complexity and Risk: The Society of Mind
07:30 Is There a Solution? The Logic of Not Building

*Roman Yampolskiy* is a Computer Scientist and professor at the University of Louisville, specializing in AI safety and cybersecurity. He is the author of _AI: Unexplainable, Unpredictable, Uncontrollable._

items.title: Arguing God from Consciousness? | Marilyn Schlitz
items.author: Closer To Truth
items.content:
<article>*Can consciousness be used to argue for the existence of God?* Research scientist and anthropologist Marilyn Schlitz examines the argument from consciousness, questioning whether our awareness of something “greater” points to a higher reality—or reflects a human construction shaped by culture and cognition.

In conversation with Robert Lawrence Kuhn, Schlitz explores how experiences of connection, transcendence, and wholeness may give rise to concepts of God, and whether this implies a supreme consciousness or a symbolic framework created to make sense of the unknown.

*Marilyn Schlitz*, Ph.D., is a research scientist, medical anthropologist, and writer on the subjects of consciousness, healing, and consciousness-based healthcare. She is currently Professor of Transpersonal Psychology at Sofia University, CEO/President Emeritus and Senior Fellow at the Institute of Noetic Sciences. 

0:00 An Obsession with God’s Existence  
0:34 The Argument from Consciousness  
1:05 Origins of Religion (Durkheim)  
2:50 Two Levels of the Argument  
4:03 God as a Human Construction  
6:07 Consciousness and Global Consciousness

items.title: What are Persons or Selves? | Michael James
items.author: Closer To Truth
items.content:
<article>*Is there a difference between the person we seem to be and what we actually are?* Michael James draws on the philosophy of Sri Ramana Maharshi to explore how Advaita Vedanta distinguishes between the person, the ego, and the self — and what that means for free will, karma, and the nature of God's action in the world.

0:00 What Is a Person? (Advaita View)
1:39 Our Real Nature: Atman and Swarupa
2:56 Ego as Entanglement
4:38 Advaita vs. Buddhism on the Self
7:09 Activity Arises with Ego
7:54 Free Will in Advaita
9:16 Karma, Predetermination, and Freedom
10:51 Karma and the Role of God
14:41 How God Acts Without Doing

*Michael James* has spent nearly 50 years studying, practicing, and translating the works of Sri Ramana Maharshi. He studied under Sri Sadhu Om, a direct disciple of Ramana, and is the author of _Atma-Vidya: The Science of Knowing Oneself._ He is regarded as a leading authority on Ramana's primary sources and experiential practice.

items.title: Is AI Safety Impossible?
items.author: Closer To Truth
items.content:
<article>Most people think AI safety is just a matter of more time and more funding. But what if it’s actually a set of unsolvable problems? 

Computer scientist Roman Yampolskiy explains why achieving total AI safety might be as impossible as building a perpetual motion machine. Is he right, or can we find a way?

#AI #AISafety #ArtificialIntelligence #RomanYampolskiy #CloserToTruth #Shorts #Technology #FutureOfAI</article>

items.title: The Mattering Instinct | Rebecca Newberger Goldstein
items.author: Closer To Truth
items.content:
<article>*We are creatures of matter who long to matter. But why?* Philosopher and novelist Rebecca Newberger Goldstein introduces her landmark new book, The Mattering Instinct: How Our Deepest Longing Drives Us and Divides Us — a bold new framework for understanding human psychology, motivation, and the ethical fault lines that divide us.

*Rebecca Newberger Goldstein* is an award-winning philosopher, writer, and public intellectual. She is the author of 10 books of acclaimed fiction and non-fiction — including 36 Arguments for the Existence of God, Betraying Spinoza, and her landmark new work The Mattering Instinct: How Our Deepest Longing Drives Us and Divides Us. She holds a PhD in philosophy of science from Princeton University and has taught at Yale, Columbia, NYU, Dartmouth, and Harvard. She is a MacArthur Fellow and recipient of the 2014 National Humanities Medal, presented by President Obama in 2015.

items.title: Is AI Control Impossible? | Roman Yampolskiy
items.author: Closer To Truth
items.content:
<article>*Can we ever guarantee that an intelligence far exceeding our own will remain aligned with human values?* AI safety expert Roman Yampolskiy discusses the "control problem," arguing that as AI evolves from simple tools into autonomous agents, our current methods of containment may be fundamentally flawed. Is superintelligence inherently uncontrollable?

*In this video, we explore:*

    * The shift from "Narrow AI" tools to autonomous agents.
    * The flaws in current AI containment strategies.
    * The "Stop Button" problem: Why a smarter system won't let you turn it off.
    * The limits of human governance in a post-human intelligence world.

1:15 From Tools to Autonomous Agents 
2:45 Why "Boxing" AI Doesn't Work

*Roman Yampolskiy* is a Computer Scientist and professor at the University of Louisville, specializing in AI safety and cybersecurity. He is the author of _AI: Unexplainable, Unpredictable, Uncontrollable._

items.title: What is God? | Michael James
items.author: Closer To Truth
items.content:
<article>*What is the true nature of the ultimate reality often called "God"?* Scholar Michael James explores the perspective of Advaita Vedanta and the teachings of Bhagavan Ramana Maharshi, proposing that God is not a separate entity or person, but infinite being, awareness, and happiness. In this deep dive, James and Robert Lawrence Kuhn contrast the "personhood" of Abrahamic traditions with the non-dual reality of pure awareness, questioning whether the finite mind can ever truly conceive the infinite.

*In this video, we explore:*

    * The definition of God as "the fullness of being."
    * Differences between the personal God of theists and the Brahman of Advaita.
    * The concept of the "ego" as a self-imposed limitation on infinite awareness.
    * The illusory nature of multiplicity and the "dream" of creation.
    * Why knowing the ultimate reality requires the surrender of individuality.

0:00 Introduction: Defining the Infinite 
1:10 Infinite Being, Awareness, and Love 
2:30 Personal God vs. Pure Awareness 
4:00 Shiva and Shakti: Power and Being 
5:50 Does God Have a Will? 
8:10 The Illusion of Multiplicity 
11:20 The Mind as the Creator 
16:15 Brahman: Saguna vs. Nirguna 
19:00 Reality and the Ego 
21:50 Surrendering Individuality

*Michael James* is a scholar and translator of the works of Bhagavan Sri Ramana Maharshi, specializing in the philosophy of Advaita Vedanta.

items.title: Can Life Be Fully Explained by Physics? Reductionism vs Emergence | Terrence Deacon
items.author: Closer To Truth
items.content:
<article>*Can the laws of physics fully explain the "aboutness" of life and morality?*

Reductionism and emergence are central to the question of whether life can be fully explained by physics and chemistry, or whether genuinely new properties arise at higher levels of organization. In this dialogue, *Terrence Deacon* argues that emergence isn't just about complexity; it’s about the unique relations and constraints that generate novel possibilities.

From the spontaneous order of thermodynamics to the future-directed nature of evolution, explore how life becomes more than the sum of its parts through a revolutionary framework of Teleodynamics.

*In this video, we explore:*

*  The Reductionism Gap: Why physics and chemistry alone can't account for "meaning."
*  The Power of Constraints: How "what doesn't happen" defines the emergence of life.
*  The Three Stages: A deep dive into Homeodynamics, Morphodynamics, and Teleodynamics.

*Chapters:*
00:00 Introduction: Reductionism vs. Emergence in Biology 
01:14 How Novelty Shows Up: Morality and "Aboutness" 
02:12 The Importance of Constraints: What Doesn't Happen 
03:06 Linguistic Capacities and the Nature of Reference 
04:10 Strong vs. Weak Emergence: The Explanatory Gap 
05:36 Beyond Top-Down and Bottom-Up Causation 
06:53 The Origins of Life: Future-Oriented Molecules 
08:01 Thermodynamics: Spontaneous Breakdown and Order 
08:52 Morphodynamics: The Dynamics of Making Forms 
09:27 Three Stages: Homeodynamics, Morphodynamics, & Teleodynamics 
10:58 End-Directed Processes in Bacteria and Viruses 
11:52 Open-Ended Evolution and the Capacity to Think

Terrence William Deacon is a Professor of Anthropology and a member of the Cognitive Science Faculty at the University of California, Berkeley. He is the author of the landmark book _Incomplete Nature: How Mind Emerged from Matter._

items.title: Can Philosophy Explain Religion—or Lead Us to God? | Richard Swinburne
items.author: Closer To Truth
items.content:
<article>Philosophy and religion intersect in questions about God, belief, and the nature of reality, raising the issue of whether reason can clarify or justify religious claims. Richard Swinburne explores how philosophical analysis approaches theological concepts such as belief, power, and personhood, and how language about God can be understood in literal, analogical, or metaphorical ways. The discussion also considers the long history of philosophy of religion, from medieval thinkers to modern debates, and whether philosophical reasoning can ultimately support belief in God.

Richard Granville Swinburne FBA is an English philosopher. He is an Emeritus Professor of Philosophy at the University of Oxford. Over the last 50 years, Swinburne has been a proponent of philosophical arguments for the existence of God.

items.title: The Historical Mary: Reconstructing the Mother of Jesus | James Tabor
items.author: Closer To Truth
items.content:
<article>The historical Mary, the mother of Jesus, is one of the most influential yet least understood figures in religious history, raising questions about her role in shaping early Christianity. James Tabor explores how historians reconstruct Mary’s life using textual analysis, chronology, and archaeology, examining sources from the New Testament and later traditions to uncover what can be known about her background, family, and social context. The discussion also considers how much can be responsibly inferred—and whether Mary may have played a deeper role in influencing the ideas and teachings attributed to Jesus.

James Tabor corrects the record, laying out the results of his intensive textual and archaeological sleuthing over the past three decades, including new evidence regarding Mary’s genealogy (which may be hiding in plain sight in the New Testament!). Tabor’s quest for the historical Mary offers a transformative perspective on Jesus and his early followers, and recovers the nature and essence of earliest Christianity.

Tabor's book, The Lost Mary, is available for purchase now: https://amzn.to/4h4dSFz

James D. Tabor is a retired professor of Religious Studies at the University of North Carolina at Charlotte, where he served as department chair for a decade. His previous ten books include the international bestseller The Jesus Dynasty, The Jesus Discovery, and Paul and Jesus. Over the past three decades Tabor has combined his study of ancient texts with field work in archaeology, and since 2008 he has been co-director of the acclaimed Mt. Zion excavation in Jerusalem. Tabor’s work has been featured in dozens of major magazines and TV documentaries, including on PBS Frontline, BBC, Discovery Channel, Nightline, 20/20, and the History Channel. He lives in Charlotte, North Carolina.

items.title: Panpsychism: Arguing Pro and Con (Part I) | Alex Gómez-Marín
items.author: Closer To Truth
items.content:
<article>Panpsychism is the idea that consciousness may exist at the most fundamental level of reality, raising the possibility that mind is far more widespread than traditionally assumed. Alex Gómez-Marín explores the arguments for and against panpsychism, including its challenge to physicalism, different forms such as micropsychism and cosmopsychism, and key objections like the combination problem. The discussion considers whether panpsychism is a necessary step toward a deeper theory of consciousness—or ultimately insufficient on its own.

Alex Gómez-Marín is a Spanish physicist turned neuroscientist. He holds a PhD in theoretical physics and a Masters in biophysics from the University of Barcelona. He was a research fellow at the EMBL-CRG Centre for Genomic Regulation and at the Champalimaud Centre for the Unknown in Lisbon. His research spans from the origins of the arrow of time in inert systems, to the neurobiology of action-perception in living organisms such as flies, worms, mice, and humans.
